# A facile solvothermal synthesis of3D magnetic MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites with enhanced peroxidase-mimicking activity and colorimetric detection of perfluorooctane sulfonate


Jing Liu a,b,1 , Jiaoyang Du a,1 , Yan Su b, ⁎ , Huimin Zhao a, ⁎

- a Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering (Ministry of Education, China), School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University

of Technology, Dalian116024, China

b Faculty of Chemical, Environmental and Biological Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian116024, China


# A R T I C L E I N F O


Keywords: MoS2nanoflowers Simple synthesis Peroxidase mimics Colorimetric sensing


# 1. Introduction


With the emergence of nanotechnology and the rise of nanomaterials, artificial enzymes based on nanomaterials, named as nanozymes, have been explored [1-6]. The prominent advantages of nanomaterials, the larger surface area, allows the enzyme mimics to expose more active sites on their surface, thus effectively improving catalytic performance. In addition, the characteristics of nanomaterials about size- and morphology-dependent catalytic properties conduct to their more flexible applications. Therefore, nanozymes are perceived as new generation artificial enzymes and further can be endowed with outstanding performance via surface modification and integration strategies except being alternatives to natural enzymes [7]. Up to now, researchers have successfully simulated a variety of natural enzymes and employed them in bioanalysis, food safety and environmental health. Especially, oxidase-like or peroxidase-mimicking properties have extensively studied, involving metal oxides, metal sulphides, noble metal, carbon-based materials and metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [8-12]. In this regard,

⁎ Corresponding authors.

E-mail addresses: susu@dlut.edu.cn (Y. Su), zhaohuim@dlut.edu.cn (H. Zhao).

1These authors contributed equally to this work.


# A B S T R A C T


Recently, two-dimensional (2D) layered structural MoS2has emerged as novel inorganic nanomaterials with excellent characteristics especially of its tunable bandgap applicable to the field of catalysis. Owing to the inherent basal surface catalytic inertia and instability of2D MoS2, methods of coupling with other functional materials or exploiting three-dimensional (3D) structure have been developed. Here, we report a facile preparation process of3D MoS2-based nanomaterials with remarkably enhanced peroxidase-like catalytic activity, magnetic separation, cost-effective and high yield, which is of great significance for the construction of colorimetric sensors. All SEM and TEM images showed a3D flower-shaped morphology of our prepared MoS2with Fe3O4nanoparticles (NPs) anchored on the 'petals', so called MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites. The absorbance plots confirmed higher catalytic property of nanocomposites, taking MoS2, Fe3O4NPs and a blend of the two by physical method as comparison. And this robust catalytic activity was utilized to achieve hypersensitive colorimetric sensing method for detecting perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) with the linear relationship between absorbance and PFOS concentrations, ranging from0.1to12.5 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was8.6nM (3σ/k).

most current nanozymes are suitable for using as a powerful tool for colorimetric detection method that being increasingly popular by virtue of point of care application, observation via naked eye, instrument-free and etc.

Recently, as a class of2D layered nanomaterials, transitional metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) have attracted much attention following graphene. Among them, 2D molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), an analogue of graphene, forms a sandwich-like structure within the layer via Mo e S covalent bonds while Van der Waals forces stack multilayers [13]. Compared with zero bandgap of graphene, 2D MoS2is of great interest due to its layer-dependent band gap. With the fewer number of layers, the transition from indirect band gap (~1.2eV) for bulk MoS2to direct band gap (~1.8eV) for single-layer MoS2occurs [14], endowing MoS2with intriguing optical properties. Moreover, especially for catalysis, direct band gap is able to facilitate electron transfer between valence band and conduction band, thus accelerating electron conductivity and improving electrocatalytic performance [15]. So far, the utilization of single or fewer-layered MoS2-based colorimetric sensor has been

explored. It is commonly believed that catalytic activity of such materials stem from the unsaturated sulfur atoms at the edge of nanosheets, while the interior plane is basically inert [15,16]. Jaiswal and coworkers [17] have made full use of the advantage of large surface area of2D MoS2, and covalently bonded with Fe3O4nanoparticles (NPs) to obtain enhanced catalytic property. Also, 2D MoS2is combined with other functional nanomaterials to improve its intrinsic properties and/ or possibly bring novel properties [18,19]. Besides, it is easy for bare2D MoS2to aggregate or reunion in solution rendering it rarely be used alone. To overcome these issues, except with addition of surfactant coatings, 3D structural nanozymes have also developed to acquire well stability and dispersity.

Owing to the intrinsic magnetism, peroxidase-like properties, stability and biocompatibility, the applications of Fe3O4NPs have always been an interesting topic [2,20-22]. To date, great efforts on coupling Fe3O4NPs with MoS2for taking advantages of each component have been devoted to various fields. Guo et al. [23] explored the catalytic activity of MoS2via fabricating Fe3O4@MoS2core-shell composites. Xue and coworkers [24] integrated MoS2nanosheets and ultrasmall Fe3O4nanoparticles as an anode for lithium ion battery application. Vinayak [25] proposed one-step synthesis method to synthesize2D MoS2/Fe3O4and achieve instant detection of glucose. Sun [26] prepared a recyclable MoS2@Fe3O4nanocomposite with remarkable photocatalytic capability. Noted that better dispersity of MoS2will be obtained in the presence of Fe3O4NPs as stabilizer against the aggregation. On the other hand, compared with loading with noble metals [27] e.g. Pt, Au and Pd, it is preferable to introducing Fe3O4NPs to design simple and practical sensing platforms with higher catalytic activity and distinctly reduced cost. However, the characteristics of nanocomposites like size, morphology, crystal structure and surface nature will be affected by synthesis methods, and further have an effect on the properties of the nanocomposites. To some extent, hydrothermal or solvothermal route has remarkable advantages that the morphology of MoS2is prone to form3D flower-like shape with enlarged surface area [28,29]. More interestingly, crystals synthesized by this kind of method are smaller and probably obtain higher catalytic activity, which are beneficial to fabricate MoS2-based sensors with reliable performance.

In this study, we synthesized MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites where Fe3O4NPs mostly grew along the edge of3D MoS2 'petals' via a solvothermal route, showing robust catalytic property towards chromogenic substrate3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to develop a typical blue color when H2O2exists. The3D MoS2nanoflowers were obtained through hydrothermal method, which was followed by the interaction with FeCl3·6H2O in ethanol solution, leading to the formation of MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites. Subsequently, the mechanism of enhanced catalytic activity was investigated from the perspectives of enzyme kinetics characteristics and Fermi level. As expected, the MoS2/ Fe3O4nanocomposites exhibit stronger affinity to substrates and higher catalytic efficiency compared to MoS2alone. Based on these findings, a simple colorimetric sensing system with hypersensitivity was established using as-prepared nanocomposites, taking the detection of PFOS as an example.


# 2. Experimental



# 2.1. Materials


Ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), thiourea (CH4N2S) and sodium acetate (CH3COONa) were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Acetic acid (CH3COOH) and ethanol absolute (CH3CH2OH) were obtained from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). 3,3,5,5-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 99%) and potassium perfluorooctanesulfonate (CF3(CF2)7SO3K, 98%) were provided by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was offered by Tianjin Bodi Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). TMB working solution was prepared by diluting its stock solution with HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3.4). The purity degree of most reagents was for analysis. Ultrapure water we used in our experiments was obtained from a Millipore water purification system (18.2MΩ·cm, Laikie Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China).


# 2.2. Apparatus


To understand the micro-morphology of simples, Field-Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi, S-4800) as well as Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2F30S-Twin) were employed. The samples for TEM were firstly dispersed to ethanol absolute, followed by10min of sonication and addition on the copper grids support. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured though Xray diffractometer (Rigaku smartlab9) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154056nm). The sample used for XRD is dry powder made by grinding. The surface elements, composition and chemical state of simples were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ThermoFisher, ESCALAB250Xi) with an Al Kα source. The absorbance determination and enzyme kinetics analysis were performed on Microplate Readers (Model Super-Max3500FL). Mott-Scottky curves were recorded on CHI660D electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Inc., Shanghai) with the usual three-electrode system (Na2SO4electrolyte (0.1M), 1k Hz).


# 2.3. Synthesis of MoS2nanoflowers


1.06g (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O and1.96g CH4N2S were added into30mL ultrapure water, and the blue uniform solution was formed under continuous magnetic stirring for30min. Then, the above acquired solution was performed hydrothermal reaction for24h at180 °C in a50mL of Teflon-lined autoclave. The products cooled to room temperature were filtered through a0.22 μm water-phase filter membrane, subsequently washed by ultrapure water three times and dried in vacuum at60 °C for12h to obtain black MoS2nanoflowers.


# 2.4. Preparation of MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites


To prepare MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites, in the presence of30mL ethanol absolute, 0.10g as-prepared MoS2nanoflowers were mixed with0.24g FeCl3·6H2O for15min for sonication which could obtain better dispersity. Then the solution was stepwise added5mL ultrapure water and0.72g CH3COONa followed by1h continuous stirring and solvothermal reaction for24h at200 °C. Products cooled to room temperature can be magnetically separated and then washed alternately with ultrapure water and ethanol three times each. The final dark brown magnetic MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites were obtained through vacuum drying at60 °C for12h.


# 2.5. Determination of peroxidase-like catalytic activity


In the general experimental method, 1.3mM TMB and200mM H2O2was successively added into aqueous solution of our prepared nanocomposites (0.06mg mL -1 ). After reaction for12min at25 °C, the absorbance values at652nm or the absorbance curves over the range of500to800nm were recorded on the Microplate Readers.


# 2.6. Detection of PFOS


At first, 10 μL aqueous solution of MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites and100 μL different concentrations of PFOS were incubated at25 °C. After30min of adsorption, 100 μL TMB (1.3mM) and20 μL H2O2 (200mM) were added into the solution. Then the reaction lasted12min at25 °C,

Fig. 1. Characterization of MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites: (a) SEM image; (b,c) TEM images at different magnifications.

and the absorbance values at652nm of the above mixed solution were recorded.


# 3. Results and discussion



# 3.1. Characterization


3D MoS2nanoflowers were obtained through hydrothermal methods. Seen from Fig. S1, the SEM image shows a3D flower shape consisting of individual nanoflakes with apparent profile. Next, MoS2/ Fe3O4nanocomposites were prepared by one-step solvothermal route. The SEM image in Fig. 1a reveals that the as-prepared nanocomposites maintain the spatial configuration of3D MoS2nanoflower. More interestingly, Fe3O4NPs with smaller diameters were selectively generated along the edge of MoS2nanoflakes. It is implied that the Fe3O4NPs would preferentially nucleate at the edges with more defective sites, which is similar to the growth mechanism of Au NPs on the MoS2or WS2 [30,31].

To further clarify the microstructure of as-prepared nanocomposites, the TEM and HRTEM were employed. The TEM image (Fig. 1b) at a selected area confirms that Fe3O4NPs were mostly concentrated at the edge of the petals and dispersed evenly. Particularly, from the HRTEM image in Fig. 1c, the highly paralleled and ordered crystal lattice on the surface can been clearly observed as well as a few layers of MoS2. Lattice spacing by measurement and calculation are as follows: 1) 0.27nm and0.704nm are indexed to (100) and (002) plane of MoS2, respectively. 2) 0.48nm, 0.26nm and0.21nm are attributed to (111), (311) and (400) plane of Fe3O4NPs, respectively, which proves the coexistence of MoS2and Fe3O4NPs in our prepared nanocomposites.

To analyze crystalline structure of as-prepared nanocomposites, XRD pattern was carried out. The diffraction peaks (Fig. 2a) located at9.5°, 32.0° and57.3° represent (002), (100), (110) plane of2H-MoS2 (JCPDS no. 37-1492), respectively. Obviously, the position of (002) reflection exhibits slightly shift, which may be related to synthesis method. According to Bragg Equation, as θ value decreases, the corresponding d spacing will increase. In this study, the precursor ammonium molybdate brought NH4 + leading to enlarge d spacing possibly, which was consistent with observation in HRTEM image that the layer spacing (0.704nm) of002plane is larger than that of bulk MoS2 (0.615nm) [32,33]. And the relatively low (002) reflection means that the3D MoS2nanoflowers in the nanocomposites have fewer layers [34,35]. Besides, the peaks corresponded to (220), (311), (400), (511) and (440) plane of Fe3O4NPs are also observed (JCPDS no.19-0629), indicating that both components have not distinct crystal form transition after combination. Furthermore, to understand the chemical states of molybdenum, iron and sulfur in as-prepared nanocomposites, XPS data were recorded (The C1s calibration is set to284.8eV). In the high-resolution Fe2p XPS spectra (Fig. 2b), the two main broad peaks located at710.9eV and724.4eV represent Fe2p3/2and Fe2p1/2, confirming two oxidation states of iron, Fe2+ and Fe3+ [36]. Likewise, the peaks at231.2eV, 228.0eV, 162.0eV and161.0eV are attributed to Mo3d3/2, Mo3d5/2, S2p1/2and S2p3/2in pure MoS2nanoflowers, respectively (Fig. 2c-d). Compared to that, both the Mo3d peaks and S2p peaks in MoS2/Fe3O4shift towards the higher binding energy by0.5eV, which implies that there exists electronic interactions between MoS2and Fe3O4 [26]. All these data show that we successfully synthesized3D structural MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites where Fe3O4NPs grew in situ on the edge of MoS2nanoflakes.


# 3.2. Peroxidase activity of MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites


In the early days, Lin et al. [37] demonstrated that MoS2nanosheets (NSs) have intrinsic but poor peroxidase-like property. On the basis of the possible synergistic effect derived from the modified MoS2nanoflakes with Fe3O4NPs, we further explored peroxidase-mimicking activity of the MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites via the decomposition of H2O2and the catalytic oxidation of TMB. As expected, the solution containing H2O2and chromogenic substrate TMB changed from colorless to distinctly blue following with the addition of as-prepared nanocomposites. On the contrary, the control experiments performed in absence of nanocomposites displayed no color change (Fig. 3a), suggesting catalytic oxidation reaction did not occur. And a typical absorption band at652nm which attribute to oxidation state of TMB (oxTMB) can be clearly observed with a concentration dependence

Fig. 2. Structure information: (a) XRD patterns of MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites and MoS2nanoflowers; (b-d) high-resolution XPS spectra of MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites including (b) Fe2p, (c) Mo3d with comparison, (d) S2p with comparison.

(Fig. 3b). The maximum absorbance was obtained when0.06mg mL -1MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites was utilized. All these results prove that the MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites can be plausible as excellent peroxidase mimics. In addition, the catalytic oxidation capability of MoS2/ Fe3O4nanocomposites towards TMB is considerably higher than that of MoS2or Fe3O4NPs alone (Fig. 3c), which confirms the fact that asprepared nanocomposites are imparted with enhanced catalytic activity by the incorporation with Fe3O4NPs. Meanwhile, the distinct color variation of corresponding solutions was recorded in the inset of Fig. 3c. However, the same experiment on a simple physical mixture of MoS2and Fe3O4NPs was performed and showed undistinguished catalytic performance, indicating that the stronger catalytic effect occurs only when the two separate components have an intense chemical interaction. Actually, natural enzymes are quite sensitive to environment and generally exhibit pH and temperature-relevant catalytic activity. As shown in Fig. S2, the effect of pH and temperature on this colorimetric reaction system have been determined. Under the condition of25 °C, the highest catalytic activity can be acquired. Meanwhile, as the pH value increases, the maximum absorbance at652nm gradually decreases and the optimal pH value of4.3was adopted for the following experiments. Furthermore, H2O2and TMB concentrations also display important roles on colorimetric reaction, thus we measured their relationship with catalytic capability. Finally, we determined that the optimal H2O2and TMB concentrations in the colorimetric reaction were200mM and1.3mM, respectively.


# 3.3. Mechanism analysis


Next, in order to figure out the kinetic mechanism of catalytic reaction of our-prepared nanocomposites, apparent steady-state kinetics was analyzed, which based on Michaelis-Menton equation:

where, Vmax refers to maximum initial velocity and [S] represents substrate concentration. Km is termed as the Michaelis constant, whose value equals to the substrate concentration when the velocity approaches to1/2Vmax. The Lineweaver-Burk curves were plotted with TMB or H2O2utilized as single variated factor (Fig. 4a and b respectively) and the significant kinetic parameters values, Km and Vmax, were obtained. The value of Km indicates the affinity of enzyme or artificial enzyme mimics to substrates, and the lower Km value represents stronger affinity and vice versa. In other words, the lower Km value guarantees further binding of active sites to specific substrates on the surface of nanomaterials. Obviously, robust catalytic activity also depends on higher Vmax value. To compare the catalytic activity, Km and Vmax value for both TMB and H2O2of HRP, Fe3O4, MoS2NSs and MoS2/ Fe3O4nanocomposites are listed, as shown in Table1. One can see that with TMB as the substrate, Km value for MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites is1.3times and1.5times lower than that of HRP and MoS2NSs alone, respectively, which indicates that MoS2/Fe3O4show higher affinity to TMB substrate. That may be attributed to the deduction that in mildly acidic condition, surface protonation of Fe3O4NPs enables it easy to bind to the hydrophilic groups (-NH2) of the TMB molecules [38,39], thus enhancing the affinity for TMB. By contrast, in case of H2O2substrate, despite MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites exhibit stronger affinity for H2O2compared with HRP, the increased Km value implies that incorporation with Fe3O4NPs worsen the affinity to H2O2compared with pristine MoS2. Thereby, relatively more H2O2was consumed to obtain maximum catalytic activity [36] which was agreement with observed phenomenon during actual experiments. But it may ensure sufficient active sites for TMB molecules. Notably, MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites

Fig. 3. Absorbance curves of (a) the colorimetric system containing TMB substrate with or without our-prepared nanocomposites, (b) different concentrations of MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites, (c) different reaction systems. Inset images of color changes (from left to right corresponding to Fe3O4, MoS2, MoS2 + Fe3O4, MoS2/ Fe3O4). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

present much higher Vmax value towards TMB substrate than that of MoS2NSs, suggesting not only the larger surface area but also the synergistic effect with Fe3O4NPs leads to the enhanced catalytic activity.

In general, TMB does not spontaneously oxidize in the presence of H2O2alone. That is because the electron energy in the none-bonding orbitals (NBO) of TMB cannot fulfil the redox potential of H2O2 [40], as is shown in Fig. S3, which makes it impossible for the long pair electrons in amino groups of TMB molecules to transfer to the lowest unoccupied molecule orbits (LUMO) of H2O2. As a result, a catalyst is often essential for electron transfer to complete catalytic oxidation reaction of TMB. Herein, we used MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites as

Fig. 4. Double reciprocal plots of kinetic parameters of MoS2/Fe3O4based on the substrate (a) TMB and (b) H2O2.

Table1Comparison of kinetic parameters towards TMB or H2O2substrate.

Fig. 5. Mott-Schottky plots of (a) MoS2, (b) MoS2/Fe3O4.

catalysts for this reaction system. It is postulated that the Fermi energy of MoS2decreased a lot after the decoration of Fe3O4NPs, following with greatly enhanced catalytic activity. For a deeper understanding of the mechanism of catalytic activity enhancement, Mott-schottky curves were measured to evaluate the Fermi levels variations of MoS2and MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites. Fig. 5a and b show that the Fermi levels of MoS2and as-prepared nanocomposites are0.28and0.46eV (vs SCE), respectively, which further verifies that decorating enable the decrease of Fermi level. MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites with lower Fermi level received the long pair electrons from NBO of TMB molecules easily, which consequently helped to speed up the reactions and reduce reaction time. Instead, the behavior of accepting electrons raised the Fermi level of nanocomposites catalyst, and then, long pair electrons filled in LUMO of H2O2 [41], completing the whole oxidation process of TMB and decomposition of H2O2.


# 3.4. Colorimetric sensing for detection of PFOS


By virtue of the robust catalytic activity MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites exhibit, a PFOS colorimetric sensor with simple-operation, lowcost and visualization was proposed (Scheme1). As noted above, since the hydroxyl groups on the surface of Fe3O4NPs were protonated under the mildly acidic condition, the sulfonate groups in PFOS were firstly attracted via electrostatic interaction, and then they may be connected in the form of hydrogen bonds [39], thus PFOS was adsorbed tightly on the surface of our prepared nanocomposites. According to the steric hindrance effect, more active sites were covered as the adsorption occurred, presenting a pale blue color in solution and showing a concentration-dependent phenomenon. That offers us an idea of quantifying PFOS by monitoring changes in absorbance at652nm, and thus fabricating a colorimetric sensing system with turn-off signal.

Under optimal conditions (pH = 3.4), different PFOS concentrations were detected after adsorbing for30min at room temperature (refer to the Experimental section). In Fig. 6a, it can be seen that the absorbance at652nm decreases with increasing the PFOS concentration, which means the catalytic oxidation capability of peroxidase mimics have been inhibited. The corresponding inset illustrates a good linear relationship that the absorbance value at652nm is in inverse proportion to logarithmic of PFOS concentration in the linear range of0.1to12.5 μM. And the linear fitting equation is A = 0.85-0.11 × log[PFOS] with the correlation coefficient (R2 ) equals to0.9948. Compared with blank triples of standard deviation, the LOD of this method was reckoned on8.6nM (3σ/k), providing a reference basis for quantification analysis of PFOS.

Relative parameters are listed in Table S1for comparison with the performance of other methods for measuring PFOS. It can be concluded that our designed sensing system is respond to PFOS over a wide range of concentrations and it has equal or higher sensitivity which is ascribe to robust peroxidase-like catalytic properties of MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites. Besides, a direct connection was built between target and enzyme mimics instead of employing with something else like dye, polyethyleneimine (PEI), etc., thus preventing the adverse influence

Scheme1. Schematic illustration of colorimetric sensing detection of PFOS.

Fig. 6. Absorbance changes at652nm (A) on the basis of PFOS concentration (the internal illustration is the fitted linear plot for PFOS detection); (B) in the presence of PFOS or other interferent (12.5 μM).

from tedious process.

Next, to explore selectivity of this simple MoS2/Fe3O4-based sensor, a series of interference experiments were carried out under the same conditions, involving possible anions and cations in water and substances similar to the structure of PFOS as interfering substances, such as Fe2+ , K + , Mg2+ , NH4 + , CO3 2- , perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), sodium dodecyl sulfonate (SDS) and sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS). Shown in Fig. 6b, it is obvious that the response of MoS2/ Fe3O4-based sensor towards most interferences is negligible with the same concentration (12.5 μM). However, in the presence of Fe2+ , the absorbance was remarkably enhanced, which indicates that Fe2+ have a promoting effect on this colorimetric reaction. That mainly because Fenton reagent composed of Fe2+ and H2O2could produce more hydroxyl radical (·OH) to oxidize TMB substrate. Also, Mg2+ shows a considerable effect. Fortunately, both two cations can be removed by cation exchange resin in practical application. On the other hand, PFOA, which is analogous to PFOS, also have similar effect on inhibiting catalytic properties of enzyme mimics. In light of the carboxyl group contained in PFOA, it is inferred that the carboxyl group also have a strong affinity for the protonated hydroxyl group, making for the total detection of PFOS and PFOA. Other similar chemicals, SDS and SDBS, containing sulfonate groups, both have considerable interference with color variation reaction, which further verifies that the electrostatic interaction between sulfonate groups and hydroxyl groups is the main driving force of this detection method. In addition, the C e F bonds in PFOS may have hydrophobic interaction with MoS2/Fe3O4nanomaterials [42], making the affinity to PFOS stronger than SDS and SDBS. In conclusion, this sensing system fabricated with MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites is capable of determining different concentrations of PFO over a certain range.


# 4. Conclusion


In summary, we have synthesized MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites accompanied with peroxidase-mimicking catalytic activities enhancement through a facile solvothermal method. A typical colorimetric reaction involving TMB and H2O2was performed, which indicates that our prepared nanocomposites indeed exhibit excellent catalytic performance than pure MoS2, Fe3O4NPs and physical mixture of the two. Furthermore, the apparent steady-state kinetic parameters, Km and Vmax, also theoretically proves MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites have stronger affinity for TMB substrate and a fast catalytic rate. Subsequently, it is verified that the decoration of Fe3O4lower the Fermi level of catalyst via Mott-schottky curves and then facilitate electron transfer, which finally promotes TMB oxidation. On the basis of the intrinsic catalytic activities, we utilized MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites to develop a simple colorimetric sensing system with relatively high sensitivity. In light of the hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions between MoS2/Fe3O4and PFOS, which leads to the adsorption of PFOS on the surface, the detection method for PFOS was constructed. The linear range and LOD are0.1to12.5 μM and8.6nM, respectively. In comparison with other measurements for PFOS, our sensing system performs equal or higher sensitivity. In addition, incorporation of recognition molecules, like aptamers, can acquire desirable specificity and selectivity for determining other specific pollutants. Consequently, owing to ease of preparation, low cost, as well as robust catalytic properties, MoS2/Fe3O4nanocomposites are competent to an excellent peroxidase mimics applied to biosensing or catalysis, which have extensive potential to fabricate novel detection sensing methods with different principles as versatile materials.
